main_actor: Hittite king Muršili I and his expeditionary army
description: The Hittite king Muršili I marched down the Euphrates and sacked Babylon, ending the First Babylonian Dynasty founded by Hammurabi's descendants. The raid looted the city and carried off the cult statues of Marduk and Ṣarpānītu, then withdrew, leaving a power vacuum that the Kassites filled. The precise year is reconstructed from synchronisms and remains debated in the Middle vs. Short Chronology dispute.
ethical_notes: Cuneiform records of the sack survive mostly from Hittite royal inscriptions and later Babylonian chronicles written centuries after the event; the Babylonian perspective on the loss of Marduk's statue is preserved only in ritual laments.

sources.citation: Beckman, G. (2000). Hittite Chronology. Akkadica 119-120, 19-32.
Telipinu Proclamation (CTH 19), Hittite royal edict (~1500 BCE).

main_actor: King Ḫammurabi of Babylon
description: Ḫammurabi promulgated a stele of 282 case laws inscribed in Akkadian cuneiform, later recovered from Susa where it had been taken as war booty by the Elamite king Shutruk-Nahhunte around 1158 BCE. The code codifies class-differentiated penalties (awīlum, muškēnum, wardum), marital and commercial law, and the lex talionis formula. It is one of the earliest extant written legal compilations though not the earliest (predated by Ur-Nammu and Lipit-Ishtar).
ethical_notes: The code's explicit hierarchy of human worth by legal class is often softened in popular summaries; a slave's life was compensated monetarily while a free man's demanded retaliation.

sources.citation: Roth, M.T. (1997). Law Collections from Mesopotamia and Asia Minor. 2nd ed. SBL.
Code of Hammurabi stele, Louvre Sb 8 (primary artifact).

main_actor: Pharaoh Khufu (Ḫwfw) and the corvée workforce of the Egyptian state
description: Construction of the Great Pyramid of Khufu, approximately 146.6 m in original height and composed of an estimated 2.3 million limestone and granite blocks. Worker villages excavated at Heit el-Ghurab demonstrate the workforce was largely conscripted Egyptian labour housed and fed by the state, not enslaved foreigners as popularly imagined. The construction span and the exact organisational methods remain partly reconstructive.
ethical_notes: Popular narratives of slave-built pyramids originate with Herodotus and were amplified in 19th-century biblical exegesis; current archaeological consensus points to rotational corvée labour from across Egypt.

sources.citation: Lehner, M. & Hawass, Z. (2017). Giza and the Pyramids. University of Chicago Press.
Tallet, P. (2017). Les papyrus de la mer Rouge I: Le journal de Merer. IFAO.

main_actor: Climate shifts, Sarasvati-Ghaggar hydrological failure, and internal de-urbanisation (no single actor)
description: The mature Harappan urban civilisation underwent progressive decline with the abandonment of Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and Dholavira. Paleoclimate data indicate monsoon weakening and the drying of the Ghaggar-Hakra river system. Earlier Aryan-invasion theories are no longer supported by archaeology; population dispersal eastward and cultural continuity into later traditions is now favoured.
silence_reason: The Indus script is undeciphered, so no indigenous textual record of the decline survives. All reconstructions depend on archaeology and paleoclimate proxies, leaving the lived experience of the collapse structurally silent.
ethical_notes: The 19th- and 20th-century Aryan-invasion framing served colonial-era racial categorisation; current scholarship has moved on but popular understanding still lags.

sources.citation: Giosan, L. et al. (2012). Fluvial landscapes of the Harappan civilization. PNAS 109(26).
Kenoyer, J.M. (1998). Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization. Oxford UP.

main_actor: Coalitions identified in Egyptian records as Peleset, Tjeker, Shekelesh, Denyen, Weshesh (ethnic composition uncertain)
description: A series of maritime and land movements by mixed populations correlated with the collapse of the Hittite Empire, the destruction of Ugarit, and attacks on Egypt repelled by Merenptah (~1207 BCE) and Ramesses III (~1177 BCE). The Sea Peoples are almost certainly a downstream symptom rather than a root cause of the Late Bronze Age collapse, which also involved drought, earthquake clusters, and systemic failure of the palace-economy network.
silence_reason: The Sea Peoples left no writing of their own; their identities, origins, and motives survive only through Egyptian propagandistic reliefs at Medinet Habu and a few Ugaritic letters written just before destruction. The collapse destroyed the very archives that could have explained it.
ethical_notes: The name 'Sea Peoples' is a modern scholarly construct coined by Emmanuel de Rougé in 1855; no ancient source uses a collective term. Using it risks reifying a heterogeneous phenomenon.

sources.citation: Cline, E.H. (2014). 1177 B.C.: The Year Civilization Collapsed. Princeton UP.
Medinet Habu inscriptions of Ramesses III (Year 8, ~1177 BCE).

main_actor: Achaean / Mycenaean coalition (attribution relies on Homeric tradition)
description: The archaeological destruction layer Troy VIIa shows a city levelled by fire with sling-stones, hastily buried bodies, and signs of siege, broadly consistent with the Homeric tradition of an Achaean siege but not proving it. Hittite texts mention Wilusa and the Ahhiyawa in the same period, supporting the historicity of a conflict zone even if the Trojan War as told by Homer is a literary condensation of multiple events.
silence_reason: The Trojan side of the conflict is entirely silent: no Wilusan archive has been recovered, and the Iliad is a Greek composition several centuries after the events, filtered through oral tradition. What the defenders thought, said, or recorded is lost.
ethical_notes: The romanticisation of the Trojan War in Western canon obscures that, archaeologically, we are looking at the extinction of a Bronze Age city whose population was killed, enslaved, or displaced.

sources.citation: Korfmann, M. (2004). Troia / Wilusa: General Background. Verlag Philipp von Zabern.
Hittite text KUB 14.3 (Tawagalawa Letter), referencing Wilusa and Ahhiyawa.

main_actor: Egyptian pharaoh Ramesses II against Hittite king Muwatalli II
description: The largest chariot battle of the ancient world, fought between approximately 20,000 Egyptians and a Hittite coalition of comparable size. Both sides claimed victory in monumental propaganda (Ramesses' 'Poem' and 'Bulletin' at Abu Simbel, Karnak, and the Ramesseum; Hittite version in the treaty archives of Ḫattuša). The outcome was tactically indecisive and strategically a Hittite win, producing c. 1259 BCE the first surviving international peace treaty between two great powers.
ethical_notes: The Egyptian account is among the earliest documented examples of state victory-propaganda; reading only Ramesses' reliefs would give a wholly inverted picture of the actual outcome.

sources.citation: Kitchen, K.A. (1982). Pharaoh Triumphant: The Life and Times of Ramesses II. Aris & Phillips.
Treaty of Kadesh, Egyptian and Hittite versions (c. 1259 BCE).

main_actor: Kushite kings Piye (Piankhy) and his successors of the 25th Dynasty
description: Piye of Kush marched north from Napata and subdued the fragmented Egyptian Delta polities, initiating the Twenty-Fifth Dynasty of Egypt. Kushite rule lasted roughly a century and produced a Nubian-Egyptian cultural florescence, ending with the Assyrian invasions of Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal that expelled Taharqa and Tantamani back to Nubia. The Kushite kings self-represented in traditional Egyptian royal iconography while retaining Nubian burial customs at El-Kurru and Nuri.
ethical_notes: 19th- and early 20th-century European scholarship minimised the 25th Dynasty as an 'interruption'; current Nubiology (Török, Morkot) treats it as a fully legitimate pharaonic dynasty with Kush as the political centre.

sources.citation: Török, L. (1997). The Kingdom of Kush: Handbook of the Napatan-Meroitic Civilization. Brill.
Victory Stele of Piye, Cairo Museum JE 48862 (c. -727).

main_actor: Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus (Nabû-naʾid)
description: Nabonidus abandoned Babylon for a decade-long self-exile at the oasis of Tayma in Arabia, leaving his son Belshazzar as regent. The motivations (devotion to the moon-god Sîn, military control of Arabian trade routes, or conflict with the Marduk priesthood) are debated. Contemporary Babylonian priestly propaganda (the Nabonidus Chronicle and the Verse Account) portrays him as heretical; these sources were curated after the Persian conquest and the priesthood's realignment with Cyrus.
silence_reason: The Marduk priesthood of Babylon effectively curated and suppressed records favourable to Nabonidus after his fall; the surviving Babylonian account is a hostile palimpsest written under Persian patronage, and Nabonidus's own perspective survives only in stelae recovered at Harran and Tayma.
ethical_notes: The Nabonidus case is a textbook example of damnatio memoriae preceded by political regime change and religious realignment; historiography must triangulate against non-Babylonian sources.

sources.citation: Beaulieu, P.-A. (1989). The Reign of Nabonidus, King of Babylon 556-539 B.C. Yale UP.
Harran stelae H1 and H2 of Nabonidus (mid-6th century BCE).

main_actor: Greek coalition led by Sparta (Leonidas) and Athens (Themistocles) against Xerxes I of Persia
description: Xerxes I invaded mainland Greece with an army conventionally estimated at 150,000-300,000 men (Herodotus's 1.7 million is universally rejected). Leonidas and roughly 7,000 Greeks delayed the Persian advance at Thermopylae; Themistocles then destroyed a substantial portion of the Persian fleet in the narrow straits of Salamis. Xerxes withdrew to Asia; the following year Plataea ended the invasion.
ethical_notes: The Persian perspective on the campaign is almost entirely absent from surviving records; Herodotus wrote for a Greek audience and the Achaemenid royal inscriptions do not dwell on the defeat.

sources.citation: Herodotus, Histories, Books VII-VIII. Loeb Classical Library.
Strauss, B. (2004). The Battle of Salamis. Simon & Schuster.

main_actor: Alexander III of Macedon and the Macedonian-Greek coalition army
description: Alexander destroyed the Achaemenid Persian Empire in a decade-long campaign (Granicus, Issus, Gaugamela), took Tyre by siege with the slaughter and enslavement of c. 30,000 inhabitants, burned Persepolis in 330 BCE, and reached the Hyphasis (Beas) in 326 BCE before his army refused to advance further. He died in Babylon in 323 BCE; the conquest produced the Hellenistic world but also mass urbicide and ethnic dislocation across the Near East.
casualties_source: Diodorus 17.46 and Arrian, Anabasis 2.24 (Tyre alone; total campaign casualties are not estimable).
ethical_notes: Western historiography traditionally frames Alexander as civiliser; Persian and Zoroastrian tradition (preserved in later Pahlavi texts such as the Ardā Wīrāz-nāmag) remembers him as Alexander the Accursed (Gujastag) who burned the Avesta and killed the magi.

sources.citation: Bosworth, A.B. (1988). Conquest and Empire: The Reign of Alexander the Great. Cambridge UP.
Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander. Loeb Classical Library.

main_actor: Chandragupta Maurya and his advisor Kautilya (Chāṇakya)
description: Chandragupta Maurya overthrew the Nanda dynasty of Magadha and, through a series of campaigns and the -303 treaty with Seleucus I Nicator (which ceded eastern Iran and the Hindu Kush for 500 war elephants), consolidated the first pan-Indian imperial polity. The administrative blueprint survives partly in the Arthaśāstra attributed to Kautilya and in Megasthenes' lost Indica preserved in Strabo and Arrian.

sources.citation: Thapar, R. (1961). Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. Oxford UP.
Megasthenes, Indica (fragments preserved in Strabo, Arrian, Diodorus).

main_actor: Mauryan emperor Ashoka (Aśoka)
description: Ashoka's conquest of Kalinga is described in his own Thirteenth Major Rock Edict: 100,000 killed, 150,000 deported, many more dead of secondary causes. The edict is extraordinary as a self-declaration by a conqueror of the moral weight of his own violence, followed by his adoption of Dhamma and patronage of Buddhism. Some scholars (notably Sanjeev Sanyal, contra Thapar) question whether the conversion was as dramatic as traditionally framed.
casualties_source: Major Rock Edict XIII (Ashoka's own count: 100,000 killed + 150,000 deported, 'many times that' dead from secondary causes).
silence_reason: The Kalingan perspective on the conquest is entirely absent; no Kalingan-authored account of the war survives, and the only narrative source is the victor's own retrospective edict.
ethical_notes: Ashoka's figures come from an imperial edict meant to dramatise his moral transformation; the numbers may be both real and rhetorical. Even so, the scale qualifies as a state-perpetrated mass killing.

sources.citation: Ashoka, Major Rock Edict XIII (Girnar, Shahbazgarhi and other copies, c. -256).
Thapar, R. (1961). Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. Oxford UP.

main_actor: Qin Shi Huangdi (Ying Zheng) and the Qin state apparatus
description: Ying Zheng of Qin, exploiting Legalist administration and mass-production military logistics, annihilated the remaining six Warring States (Han, Zhao, Wei, Chu, Yan, Qi) between -230 and -221 and proclaimed himself Shi Huangdi, 'First Emperor'. The unification produced standardised script (小篆), weights, measures, axle-widths, and the abolition of feudal fiefs in favour of 36 commanderies governed by centrally appointed officials.
ethical_notes: Han-dynasty sources (notably Sima Qian) are hostile to Qin and likely exaggerate its cruelty to justify their own legitimacy; recent archaeology at Liye and Shuihudi has complicated the pure-tyranny narrative.

sources.citation: Sima Qian, Shiji, Basic Annals of Qin (史記·秦本紀). Han-era compilation.
Lewis, M.E. (2007). The Early Chinese Empires: Qin and Han. Harvard UP.

main_actor: Qin Shi Huangdi acting on counsel of chancellor Li Si
description: By imperial decree, all privately held copies of the Shijing (Classic of Poetry), Shujing (Book of Documents), and the philosophical writings of the Hundred Schools outside the Qin state archives were to be surrendered and burned, on pain of tattooing and penal labour. The following year, several hundred scholars accused of criticising the throne were reportedly buried alive (坑儒). The 'burying of the scholars' component is treated with growing scepticism by modern sinology as a later Han embellishment.
casualties_source: Sima Qian, Shiji 6 (figure for scholars buried; widely contested).
silence_reason: The targeted works were systematically destroyed; what we have of pre-Qin literature survived only through state archives, oral transmission, and later reconstruction. The epistemic scale of what was lost cannot be measured, because we cannot know what we cannot read.
ethical_notes: Book-burnings are framed by the succeeding Han as the defining Qin crime; the real scholarly consensus is that the text destruction was real but less comprehensive than the propaganda suggests, and the scholar-killing is likely exaggerated.

sources.citation: Sima Qian, Shiji, chapter 6 (秦始皇本紀).
Petersen, J.O. (1995). Which Books Did the First Emperor of Ch'in Burn? Monumenta Serica 43.

main_actor: Hannibal Barca commanding the Carthaginian and allied army
description: Hannibal's double envelopment of a numerically superior Roman army under consuls Lucius Aemilius Paullus and Gaius Terentius Varro produced one of the bloodiest single-day battles in premodern history. Polybius reports 70,000 Roman dead, Livy 48,000; modern estimates cluster around 50,000-70,000 killed and c. 10,000 captured. Rome refused to surrender, and the battle paradoxically confirmed the eventual trajectory of the Second Punic War.
casualties_source: Polybius, Histories 3.117 (70,000) vs. Livy, Ab Urbe Condita 22.49 (48,200).
ethical_notes: Carthaginian sources on Cannae do not survive; our narrative depends entirely on the culture that lost the battle but won the war and subsequently erased the other side.

sources.citation: Polybius, Histories, Book III. Loeb Classical Library.
Daly, G. (2002). Cannae: The Experience of Battle in the Second Punic War. Routledge.

main_actor: Roman army under Publius Cornelius Scipio Aemilianus, by decree of the Roman Senate
description: After a three-year siege, Carthage was taken street by street over six days of urban combat. Appian reports 50,000 survivors enslaved out of a pre-siege population estimated between 200,000 and 500,000; the city was razed, the site ritually cursed, and the territory annexed as the province of Africa. The destruction was deliberate, planned (Cato's 'Carthago delenda est'), and aimed at the political, cultural, and demographic elimination of a peer civilisation; by modern legal definitions (UN 1948) it meets the intent criterion for genocide.
casualties_source: Appian, Punica 130-131 (50,000 enslaved; pre-siege population estimates from Strabo, Hoyos 2010).
silence_reason: The destruction was total enough that almost no Punic literature, historiography, religious texts, or administrative records survived; what we know of Carthage was written by those who destroyed it. This is a textbook case of ETHICS-008 structural silence imposed by the victor.
ethical_notes: Classifying this as genocide rather than the traditional 'destruction of Carthage' aligns with the explicit senatorial intent to eliminate Carthage as a people and with the scale of killing and enslavement. The traditional framing as a 'victory' reflects the survival of only the Roman perspective.

sources.citation: Appian, Roman History, Book 8 (Libyca/Punica). Loeb Classical Library.
Hoyos, D. (2010). The Carthaginians. Routledge.
Le Bohec, Y. (2014). La troisième guerre punique. Ausonius.

main_actor: Roman army under consul Lucius Mummius Achaicus
description: After defeating the Achaean League, Mummius took Corinth, killed the adult male population, enslaved women and children, looted the art collection (sent to Rome to adorn triumphs), and burned the city. The site remained largely abandoned for a century until Julius Caesar's colonia Laus Iulia Corinthiensis in -44. The destruction was contemporary with Carthage's, marking -146 as the consolidation year of Roman Mediterranean hegemony through twin urbicides.
ethical_notes: Pausanias's later account (2nd c. CE) mourns the lost Corinthian art as aesthetic tragedy; the human toll was barely registered by the sources that survived.

sources.citation: Pausanias, Description of Greece 7.16.
Wiseman, J. (1979). Corinth and Rome I: 228 B.C.-A.D. 267. ANRW II.7.1.

main_actor: Roman army of Titus Flavius Vespasianus (the future emperor Titus)
description: After a five-month siege, Titus took Jerusalem, destroyed the Second Temple on 9-10 Av, and reduced the city. Flavius Josephus, an eyewitness serving on the Roman side, reports 1.1 million killed and 97,000 enslaved; Tacitus gives 600,000 defenders. Modern estimates place dead at 250,000-600,000 including famine casualties. The Temple destruction is a foundational trauma in rabbinic Judaism and the cause of the definitive geographic dispersal of Judean religious authority.
casualties_source: Josephus, Jewish War 6.420 (1.1M + 97,000 enslaved); modern revision in Goodman (2007).
ethical_notes: Josephus wrote under Flavian patronage and his numbers serve partly as triumphal rhetoric; nonetheless the archaeological destruction layer and the collapse of Temple Judaism are unambiguous.

sources.citation: Flavius Josephus, The Jewish War, Books V-VI. Loeb Classical Library.
Goodman, M. (2007). Rome and Jerusalem: The Clash of Ancient Civilizations. Allen Lane.

description: A Plinian eruption on approximately 24-25 October 79 CE (the traditional 24 August date is now contested by carbon-dated graffiti and organic remains pointing to autumn). Pyroclastic surges buried Pompeii, Herculaneum, Oplontis, and Stabiae; Pliny the Younger's two letters to Tacitus are the earliest systematic eyewitness account of such an eruption, describing his uncle Pliny the Elder's death during the evacuation effort from Misenum.
casualties_source: Recovered remains to date plus population estimates for buried settlements (Sigurdsson 2002).

sources.citation: Pliny the Younger, Letters 6.16 and 6.20 (to Tacitus).
Sigurdsson, H. et al. (2002). The Archaeology of Vesuvius. In Encyclopedia of Volcanoes.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ

location_name: Roman Empire (brought from Mesopotamia by returning legions)
description: A pandemic, most likely smallpox (variola) on the basis of Galen's clinical description, swept the Roman Empire after legions returned from the Parthian campaign of Lucius Verus. Mortality estimates range from 5 to 10 million, representing 7-10 percent of the empire's population; the emperor Lucius Verus himself died in 169, and Marcus Aurelius in 180 probably of the same disease. The epidemic marks the beginning of sustained demographic pressure on the later Principate.
casualties_source: Littman & Littman (1973). Galen and the Antonine Plague. AJP 94(3).
ethical_notes: The pathogen identification is inferred from Galen's clinical descriptions (no aDNA confirmation); smallpox is most probable but measles and other agents have been proposed.

sources.citation: Galen, De methodo medendi and scattered clinical references.
Harper, K. (2017). The Fate of Rome: Climate, Disease, and the End of an Empire. Princeton UP.

main_actor: Zhang Jue (Zhang Jiao) and the Taiping Dao movement
description: Zhang Jue's millenarian Taiping Dao (Way of Great Peace) mobilised peasant followers wearing yellow headscarves against the Han state under the slogan 'The Azure Sky is dead; the Yellow Sky will rise' (蒼天已死，黃天當立). The rebellion was crushed within a year but the regional warlord armies (Cao Cao, Yuan Shao, Liu Bei) raised to fight it never demobilised, accelerating the terminal decline of Han central authority. Casualty estimates are extremely uncertain; Hou Hanshu figures are orders-of-magnitude approximations.
ethical_notes: The Taiping Dao's own writings are lost; the rebellion is known almost entirely through the hostile lens of Fan Ye's Hou Hanshu and later Three Kingdoms historiography.

sources.citation: Fan Ye, Hou Hanshu, 後漢書·皇甫嵩列傳. 5th c. compilation.
de Crespigny, R. (2007). A Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to the Three Kingdoms. Brill.

main_actor: Emperor Constantine I convening approximately 220-318 bishops
description: The First Ecumenical Council, convened and presided over politically by Constantine, condemned Arianism, fixed the Nicene Creed affirming the consubstantiality (ὁμοούσιος) of Father and Son, and standardised the date of Easter. The council marked the transformation of Christianity from persecuted sect to instrument of imperial cohesion and initiated the institutional suppression of non-Nicene Christianities, including the burning of Arius's writings.
silence_reason: Arius's own writings and those of the condemned minority were ordered destroyed by imperial edict; Arian theology survives largely through the polemics of its opponents (Athanasius, Socrates Scholasticus), a classic ETHICS-008 suppression.
ethical_notes: The council is typically narrated from the winning Nicene perspective; the losing parties (Arius, Eusebius of Nicomedia) are voiced only through hostile paraphrase.

sources.citation: Eusebius of Caesarea, Vita Constantini, Book III.
Ayres, L. (2004). Nicaea and Its Legacy. Oxford UP.

main_actor: Aksumite king Ezana (ʿEzana) and the Aksumite army
description: Ezana of Aksum campaigned against Meroë and effectively ended the Meroitic kingdom as an independent polity; an inscription in Ge'ez, Greek, and Sabaic at Aksum records the campaign against the Noba and Kasu. The dating and the question of whether Meroë had already collapsed by the time of Ezana's campaign are debated. Ezana also converted to Christianity around the same period, aligning Aksum with the late Roman religious sphere.
silence_reason: Meroitic script remains only partially deciphered; the Kushite/Meroitic side of the account is structurally silent. Reconstruction depends on Ezana's own trilingual inscription and on archaeology, not on any indigenous narrative.
ethical_notes: Historiography long conflated the end of Meroitic political independence with civilisational collapse; recent work (Edwards, Welsby) shows continuity of Nubian population and culture into the post-Meroitic kingdoms of Nobatia, Makuria and Alodia.

sources.citation: Ezana Stone trilingual inscription, Aksum (c. 350 CE).
Welsby, D. (1998). The Kingdom of Kush: The Napatan and Meroitic Empires. British Museum Press.

main_actor: Visigothic king Alaric I and his army
description: Alaric's Visigoths entered Rome on 24 August 410 and sacked the city for three days. Physical destruction was limited compared to later sacks (Alaric ordered Christian basilicas spared as asylum), but the symbolic blow was immense: the first fall of Rome to a foreign army in nearly 800 years provoked Jerome's grief-stricken letters and Augustine's De Civitate Dei, written to rebut pagan attributions of the catastrophe to Christian abandonment of the old gods.
ethical_notes: The Visigothic perspective is preserved only through Greek and Latin authors (Olympiodorus, Orosius, Jordanes); Alaric's diplomatic grievances (land and grain for his foederati) are legible in these hostile sources but were not recorded by Gothic authors contemporaneously.

sources.citation: Orosius, Historiae adversum Paganos 7.39.
Heather, P. (2005). The Fall of the Roman Empire. Macmillan.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Gregorian equivalent may differ
